title: Simple Hall Table
summary: A narrow entry table built entirely from 1x2s and 1x3s, nailed and glued rather than pocket-holed — a genuinely beginner-friendly build with breadboard ends on the top.
description:
This hall table is built almost entirely with a hammer or nailer and glue rather than pocket holes, which makes it one of the more genuinely beginner-accessible tables in this catalog. The legs are built from a 1x3 and 1x2 nailed together at 90°, giving each leg an L-shaped cross-section that's stiffer than a single flat board while using only construction lumber.

Build from the bottom up: side supports first, marked a consistent 5" up from the floor, then the center stretcher (kept centered but with its top edge flush to the support), then the side trim pieces run down the stretcher's length. Keep every top edge flush as you go — this plan is a chain of flush-edge references, not a single measured layout, so a small drift early on compounds by the final steps.

The top overhangs 4" on each side beyond the legs, with breadboard ends capping the front and back — and the plan calls out a genuinely good habit for the very last piece: **don't cut the final trim piece to a plan measurement, measure your own assembled base and cut to that.** After four nailed-and-glued assemblies stacked in sequence, the real final dimension is rarely the one on paper.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Do not pre-cut — measure your assembled top and cut to fit exactly.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four leg fronts (A) at 29-1/4", two side supports (C) at 10" and two side trim (E) at 37" from the 1x3. Cut four leg sides (B) at 29-1/4" and one stretcher (D) at 37" from the 1x2. Cut two side apron (F) at 10", two front and back apron (G) at 37", two breadboard ends (I) at 11-1/2" and one final breadboard end (J) at 41" from the 1x4. Cut one tabletop base (H) at 48" from the 1x12. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 1x3, two 8 ft 1x2, two 8 ft 1x4 and one 8 ft 1x12. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the legs
steps.body: Nail through the 1x3 leg fronts into the edge of the 1x2 leg sides with 2" nails and glue, positioning the 1x3s to the front and 1x2s to the side, forming an L-shaped leg.

steps.title: Add the side supports
steps.body: Mark each leg 5" from the bottom for the side support's position. Nail the side supports to the legs from the inside with 1-1/4" nails and glue.

steps.title: Add the stretcher and side trim
steps.body: Center the stretcher on the side support, keeping top edges flush, and nail through the support into the stretcher's end with glue and 2" nails. Nail the side trim through the supports into the trim, then down the stretcher's length, keeping the stretcher's top edge perfectly flush with the trim's top edge.

steps.title: Add the aprons
steps.body: Nail the side, front, and back apron pieces to the legs using 1-1/4" nails and glue, keeping top edges flush. Check for square.

steps.title: Add the top and breadboard ends
steps.body: Attach the tabletop base, keeping front and back edges flush with the legs and letting the sides overhang 4" on either side. Nail into the legs and aprons with 2" nails and glue. Attach the two breadboard ends with 1-1/4" nails and glue, keeping all outside edges flush.

steps.title: Fit the final trim piece and finish
steps.body: Measure your assembled base for the final trim piece's exact length rather than cutting to the plan number, then attach with glue and 1-1/4" nails. Sand and finish the whole table.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

unresolvedImages.alt: Narrow entryway hall table with breadboard ends
